---
title: "DEV_P012: Reactive Data Filtering Principle"
subtitle: "Applications should use reactive data filtering to efficiently process large datasets, applying filters as early as possible in the data processing pipeline"
chapter: "CH03"
category: "principle"
number: "DEV_P012"
date-created: "2025-04-03"
date-modified: "2025-08-17"
author: "Claude"
type: "principle"
law: "Development Methodology Law"
article: 
  number: 12
  title: "Reactive Data Filtering Principle"
related_to:
  - "DEV_P001: Debug Principles"
  - "DM_R003: Database Access Pattern"
  - "UI_P003: Sidebar Filtering Only"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
    number-sections: true
---

# Article 12: Reactive Data Filtering Principle {#overview}

**Development Methodology Law Article 12 (Reactive Data Filtering Principle)**

Applications should use reactive data filtering to efficiently process large datasets, applying filters as early as possible in the data processing pipeline to ensure only necessary data is processed.

:::{.callout-important}
## Core Principle
Implement reactive filtering at the data source level before performing calculations or visualizations to improve application performance and responsiveness.
:::

# Section 1: Rationale {#rationale}

## Subsection 1: Benefits of Reactive Filtering {#benefits}

Implementing early reactive filtering provides:

1. **Performance Enhancement**: Reduces computation on unnecessary data
2. **Responsiveness**: Maintains UI responsiveness even with large datasets
3. **Scalability**: Enables applications to handle growing datasets
4. **Resource Efficiency**: Minimizes memory consumption
5. **User Experience**: Ensures consistent performance across varying data sizes

## Subsection 2: Performance Optimization Principles {#performance-principles}

Reactive filtering optimizes performance through these mechanisms:

- **Data Volume Reduction**: Early filtering significantly reduces subsequent processing volume
- **Computation Optimization**: Only executes aggregations and statistics on relevant data
- **Memory Management**: Avoids loading entire datasets into memory
- **Response Time**: Reduces user wait times
- **Resource Allocation**: More efficient use of system resources

# Section 2: Implementation Guidelines {#implementation}

## Subsection 1: Filter-First Architecture {#filter-first-architecture}

Establish reactive filtered datasets as the foundation for all subsequent operations:

```r
# Create reactive filtered dataset
filtered_data <- reactive({
  # Apply filters to raw dataset
  result <- raw_data[raw_data$category == input$category_filter, ]
  
  # Additional filter conditions
  if (!is.null(input$search) && input$search != "") {
    result <- result[grepl(input$search, result$name, fixed = TRUE), ]
  }
  
  # Date range filtering
  if (!is.null(input$date_range)) {
    result <- result[
      result$date >= input$date_range[1] & 
      result$date <= input$date_range[2], 
    ]
  }
  
  return(result)
})

# Use filtered data for all outputs
output$plot <- renderPlot({
  # Use already filtered data
  ggplot(filtered_data(), aes(x = value, y = count)) + 
    geom_point() +
    theme_minimal()
})

output$summary_table <- renderDataTable({
  # Same filtered data for table
  datatable(filtered_data(), options = list(pageLength = 25))
})
```

## Subsection 2: Progressive Loading Strategy {#progressive-loading}

Implement staged data loading to optimize initial load times:

### Initial Loading Optimization {#initial-loading}

```r
# Initially load only essential data
initial_data <- reactive({
  # Load basic columns and recent records
  tbl2(conn, "main_table") %>%
    filter(created_date >= Sys.Date() - 30) %>%  # Last 30 days
    select(id, name, category, value, created_date) %>%
    collect()
})

# Detailed data loaded on demand
detailed_data <- reactive({
  req(input$selected_id)
  
  tbl2(conn, "main_table") %>%
    filter(id == input$selected_id) %>%
    collect()
})
```

### Pagination and Incremental Loading {#pagination-incremental}

```r
# Implement paginated loading
paginated_data <- reactive({
  page_size <- 100
  offset <- (input$page_number - 1) * page_size
  
  filtered_query <- tbl2(conn, "large_table") %>%
    filter(
      category %in% input$selected_categories,
      date >= input$start_date
    )
  
  # Paginated query
  filtered_query %>%
    arrange(date) %>%
    slice(offset + 1:(offset + page_size)) %>%
    collect()
})
```

## Subsection 3: Computation Optimization Patterns {#computation-optimization}

### Post-Filter Aggregation {#filtered-aggregation}

```r
# Perform aggregation calculations on filtered data
summary_stats <- reactive({
  data <- filtered_data()
  
  list(
    total_count = nrow(data),
    average_value = mean(data$value, na.rm = TRUE),
    category_breakdown = data %>%
      group_by(category) %>%
      summarise(
        count = n(),
        avg_value = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE),
        .groups = "drop"
      )
  )
})
```

### Result Caching {#result-caching}

```r
# Cache results of expensive computations
cached_analysis <- reactive({
  # Build cache key
  cache_key <- paste(
    input$category_filter,
    input$date_range[1],
    input$date_range[2],
    sep = "_"
  )
  
  # Check cache
  if (exists(paste0("cache_", cache_key), envir = .GlobalEnv)) {
    return(get(paste0("cache_", cache_key), envir = .GlobalEnv))
  }
  
  # Perform computation
  result <- perform_complex_analysis(filtered_data())
  
  # Store in cache
  assign(paste0("cache_", cache_key), result, envir = .GlobalEnv)
  
  return(result)
})
```

## Subsection 4: Result Size Limits {#result-size-limits}

Implement reasonable result set size limits:

```r
# Apply result size limits
limited_results <- reactive({
  data <- filtered_data()
  max_rows <- 1000
  
  if (nrow(data) > max_rows) {
    # Display warning message
    showNotification(
      paste("Showing first", max_rows, "results of", nrow(data), "total"),
      type = "warning",
      duration = 5
    )
    
    # Return limited results
    return(data[1:max_rows, ])
  }
  
  return(data)
})
```

## Subsection 5: Efficient Text Search {#efficient-text-search}

Implement optimized text search mechanisms:

```r
# Efficient text search implementation
text_filtered_data <- reactive({
  base_data <- category_filtered_data()  # Apply other filters first
  
  if (is.null(input$search_text) || input$search_text == "") {
    return(base_data)
  }
  
  search_term <- trimws(tolower(input$search_text))
  
  # Use fixed string matching for better performance
  if (nchar(search_term) > 0) {
    # Pre-compute lowercase versions for case-insensitive search
    base_data$name_lower <- tolower(base_data$name)
    base_data$description_lower <- tolower(base_data$description)
    
    # Perform search
    matches <- grepl(search_term, base_data$name_lower, fixed = TRUE) |
               grepl(search_term, base_data$description_lower, fixed = TRUE)
    
    result <- base_data[matches, ]
    
    # Clean up temporary columns
    result$name_lower <- NULL
    result$description_lower <- NULL
    
    return(result)
  }
  
  return(base_data)
})
```

# Section 3: Performance Optimization Guidelines {#performance-guidelines}

## Subsection 1: Database-Level Optimization {#database-optimization}

### Indexing Strategy {#indexing-strategy}

Ensure proper indexes exist for commonly filtered columns:

```sql
-- Create indexes for commonly filtered columns
CREATE INDEX idx_category ON main_table(category);
CREATE INDEX idx_date_range ON main_table(created_date);
CREATE INDEX idx_status ON main_table(status);

-- Composite indexes for multi-column filtering
CREATE INDEX idx_category_date ON main_table(category, created_date);
```

### Query Optimization {#query-optimization}

Push filtering logic to the database level:

```r
# Database-side filtering
db_filtered_data <- reactive({
  tbl2(conn, "large_table") %>%
    filter(
      category %in% !!input$selected_categories,
      created_date >= !!input$start_date,
      created_date <= !!input$end_date,
      status == !!input$status_filter
    ) %>%
    collect()  # Only bring data into R when necessary
})
```

## Subsection 2: Pre-Aggregation Strategy {#pre-aggregation}

Pre-compute aggregations for common queries:

```r
# Pre-computed summary statistics
pre_computed_summary <- reactive({
  # Check for pre-computed summary tables
  summary_table <- tryCatch({
    tbl2(conn, "daily_summary") %>%
      filter(
        date >= !!input$start_date,
        date <= !!input$end_date
      ) %>%
      collect()
  }, error = function(e) {
    # Fallback to real-time computation
    db_filtered_data() %>%
      group_by(date = as.Date(created_date)) %>%
      summarise(
        total_count = n(),
        avg_value = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE),
        .groups = "drop"
      )
  })
  
  return(summary_table)
})
```

## Subsection 3: Sampling Strategy {#sampling-strategy}

Consider data sampling for exploratory visualizations:

```r
# Smart sampling
sampled_data <- reactive({
  full_data <- filtered_data()
  max_sample_size <- 5000
  
  if (nrow(full_data) > max_sample_size) {
    # Stratified sampling to maintain representativeness
    if ("category" %in% names(full_data)) {
      sampled <- full_data %>%
        group_by(category) %>%
        sample_n(min(n(), max_sample_size / n_distinct(full_data$category))) %>%
        ungroup()
    } else {
      # Simple random sampling
      sampled <- full_data[sample(nrow(full_data), max_sample_size), ]
    }
    
    showNotification(
      paste("Using", nrow(sampled), "samples for visualization (of", nrow(full_data), "total)"),
      type = "info"
    )
    
    return(sampled)
  }
  
  return(full_data)
})
```

# Section 4: Incremental Update Patterns {#incremental-updates}

## Subsection 1: Smart Data Updates {#smart-data-updates}

Avoid recomputing entire datasets when only portions change:

```r
# Incremental data updates
incremental_data <- reactiveVal()

# Initial load
observeEvent(input$initial_load, {
  initial <- load_initial_data()
  incremental_data(initial)
})

# Incremental updates
observeEvent(input$add_filter, {
  current_data <- incremental_data()
  
  # Only update affected portions
  if (input$filter_type == "category") {
    updated_data <- current_data[current_data$category %in% input$new_categories, ]
  } else {
    # Other filter logic
    updated_data <- apply_incremental_filter(current_data)
  }
  
  incremental_data(updated_data)
})
```

## Subsection 2: Selective Recomputation {#selective-recomputation}

```r
# Conditional reactive
conditional_reactive <- reactive({
  # Only recompute when specific inputs change
  if (input$major_filter_change > 0) {
    # Full recalculation
    return(full_recalculation())
  } else {
    # Use cached results
    return(cached_results())
  }
}) %>% bindEvent(input$major_filter_change, ignoreNULL = FALSE)
```

# Section 5: Exceptions and Special Cases {#exceptions}

## Subsection 1: Authorized Exceptions {#authorized-exceptions}

Exceptions are permitted in these cases:

### Small Datasets {#small-datasets}

```r
# When total dataset size is manageable
data_processing_strategy <- reactive({
  total_rows <- get_total_row_count()
  
  if (total_rows < 1000) {
    # Small dataset: load all data
    return("load_all")
  } else if (total_rows < 100000) {
    # Medium dataset: simple filtering
    return("simple_filter")
  } else {
    # Large dataset: complex filtering and pagination
    return("complex_filter")
  }
})
```

### Complex Data Relationships {#complex-relationships}

```r
# When filtering would break data relationships
relational_data <- reactive({
  if (input$maintain_relationships) {
    # Maintain complete dataset to preserve relationships
    return(load_complete_relational_data())
  } else {
    # Apply standard filtering
    return(filtered_data())
  }
})
```

### Special Visualization Requirements {#special-visualizations}

```r
# Specific visualizations require complete datasets
visualization_data <- reactive({
  if (input$chart_type %in% c("distribution", "correlation_matrix")) {
    # These charts need complete datasets
    return(complete_dataset())
  } else {
    # Other charts can use filtered data
    return(filtered_data())
  }
})
```

## Subsection 2: Exception Documentation {#exception-documentation}

Document all performance exceptions:

```r
# Performance exception logging
log_performance_exception <- function(reason, data_size, processing_time) {
  cat(sprintf(
    "PERFORMANCE EXCEPTION: %s\n",
    "Reason: %s\n",
    "Data Size: %d rows\n", 
    "Processing Time: %.2f seconds\n\n"
  ), reason, data_size, processing_time)
}
```

# Section 6: Testing and Monitoring {#testing-monitoring}

## Subsection 1: Performance Testing {#performance-testing}

Regularly test filtering performance:

```r
# Performance benchmark testing
benchmark_filtering <- function() {
  test_sizes <- c(1000, 10000, 100000, 1000000)
  
  results <- map_dfr(test_sizes, function(size) {
    test_data <- generate_test_data(size)
    
    # Test filtering performance
    timing <- system.time({
      filtered <- test_data[test_data$category == "A", ]
    })
    
    data.frame(
      data_size = size,
      filter_time = timing["elapsed"],
      memory_used = object.size(filtered)
    )
  })
  
  return(results)
}
```

## Subsection 2: Performance Monitoring {#performance-monitoring}

Implement real-time performance monitoring:

```r
# Performance monitoring functionality
monitor_performance <- reactive({
  start_time <- Sys.time()
  
  # Execute filtering operation
  result <- filtered_data()
  
  end_time <- Sys.time()
  processing_time <- as.numeric(end_time - start_time)
  
  # Log performance metrics
  performance_log <- data.frame(
    timestamp = Sys.time(),
    operation = "data_filtering",
    data_size = nrow(result),
    processing_time = processing_time,
    memory_usage = object.size(result)
  )
  
  # Alert for slow queries
  if (processing_time > 2) {
    showNotification(
      paste("Query processing time:", round(processing_time, 2), "seconds"),
      type = "warning"
    )
  }
  
  return(list(data = result, performance = performance_log))
})
```

# Conclusion {#conclusion}

The Reactive Data Filtering Principle ensures that Shiny applications can efficiently handle large datasets by implementing smart filtering at the early stages of the data processing pipeline. Through filter-first architecture, progressive loading, pre-aggregation, and intelligent caching strategies, applications can maintain excellent performance and user experience across various data sizes.

---

*Based on: Development Methodology Law Article 12*  
*Related to: [DEV_P001 Debug Principles](DEV_P001_debug_principles.qmd), [DM_R003 Database Access Pattern](../../CH02_data_management/rules/DM_R003_database_access_tbl.qmd)*
